item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image's closest source, found here, shows no indication it has been released under Creative Commons, and the second image could not be sourced, assumed non-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I was expecting at least a subtle allusion to it somewhere :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, mostly for the dramatic effectiveness of the repetition of "Send more Builders."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Deserves a +1 for the scp and a second one for the interview. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but it's a bit too similar to the Broken God for me to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To all the posters complaining about this being physics defying, here's an argument for how this could work. (Ironic, since normally i'm objecting to physics defiance).
You're all asuming that the lightning is simply being subjected to the normal elecryodynamics we know. But this is, of course, not normal lightning, since normal lighning doesn't induce compulsions like that (at least not typically). So lets define a property of this abnormality. Let's say that the lightning is subject to another field force, outside of the known. We'll call this the skippy force, because why the hell not. So, forces are defined by their field potential, with a need to move from higher potential to lower potential. now, we could say that all lightning is subject to the skippy force, and just assume that the skippy field is essentially flat and uniform. this wouldn't be implausible, and there are existing examples. Alternatively, the skippy field could vary more, and this particular lightning is just rendered subject to it.

So how does this help. Well, lets just say this entity is capable of shaping the skippy field under its area of influence. What it's doing is creating areas of low enough skippy potential to form channels, which redirrect the lightning to follow them, and thus hit the target. It simply needs to create intense enough skippy potentials to override the effects of the electric potential. And viola. Redirected lightning!

Top those who might argue against this whole skippy potential idea, i would invite you to look into the Higgs boson. Current theory says mass generation comes form the presence of Higgs potential. We didn;t know about this potential at all for years. And even now we're struggling to prove it. Another new potential out there? Totally plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My headcanon is that this is the "spirit" of the Broken God, and it's trying to get more people to build its body so it can return.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is a Brain Storm…
That's lost here…

I guess a mind wandered and never came back!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if the storm targets cars, there's no requirement that it has any special results—the storm sees a hunk of metal, it targets it, the person inside is normally fine, and all is well with the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They aren't being administered amnesiacs to cure them of anything. They're a potential security risk, and the amnesiacs are there to solve that problem. Be glad the amnesiacs aren't lead-based and administered through a firearm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :o… i live in venezuela (relatively close of the place catatumbos occurs)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: UUUUUUUUUUUUUUGH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said, anything other than tallest/most conductive target would still be physics-defying magic, and there really is no way around that. I'm just trying to think of how it could be made easier for the reader to suspend disbelief.

Everyone knows that cars are a safe place during an electrical storm, I think they taught that in kindergarten or something. So specifically mentioning that it targets cars to get to drivers is more jarring than saying it targets houses to get to occupants, even though almost any building is better protection from lightning than a car.

Looking at it a day later, I agree that my idea would not be the way to go; it just calls more attention to the odd behavior. Hmmm, maybe just cut the cars altogether?…I dunno. Nearly 50 people (including myself) like it well enough as it is to up-vote, so I'm probably trying to fix a problem that doesn't need solving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…I just realized what this is.

+ show block
– hide block

brainstorming

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since I think it's pretty well established that this is magic lightning, I have no inherent problem with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Electricity flows like water, basically. Except with potential instead of hight. What you're describing is the equivalent of water, instead of flowing down a hill, going up it, through a complicated maze it has no way of knowing the nature of, and then assembling a computer. Which is fine, but just be aware how extremely magical that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As ChazzK notes, permanent lightning storms are an actual phenomenon that occur in several places on Earth; the picture I used for the article is of the Catatumbo lightning, which has been going on in Venezuela at least since the 16th century. I think it can be safely assumed that the Foundation is suppressing the publication of any research regarding this particular storm, but I get enough comments about my articles being too wordy so I didn't want to go into too much detail about that either.

Since it's been brought up a few times, I am not a mechanic and I personally didn't know before writing this that being inside a car during a lightning storm is safe. Again, I think it can be assumed that the lightning, being intelligent and having a desire to target humans, manages to strike the car in just the right spot so that the electricity comes up through the dashboard instruments, or the chrome on the gearshift, or something of that nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of people have mentioned the car=protected thing, I think I have a possible solution that stops short of it being completly autonomous electricity. It's already established that the lightning can pick its targets, favoring people over taller objects. If you point out that it's targeting some particular part of the car/house that would channel into the person it might forestall any objections. Such as, it specifically strikes the car antenae, which channels into the radio system, then electrical, then ignition, then the steering column and driver.

Or not, that would still be magical smart lightning. I just prefer my magic with a nice thick coating of techno-babble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Although not completely understood, [PLACE NAME HERE] has shown a surprising congregation of meteorological aspects that lends it a semi-unique phenomena; it is the home to a near-permanent lightning storm that only occasionally drifts outwards to nearby, more heavily settled areas."

Considering that's how the REAL LIFE phenomena in at least one or two locations is explained…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's probably impossible. The thing is there; it's perfectly visible. You can't stop weather satellites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any persons surviving an encounter with SCP-1688 are to be debriefed and administered Class-A or Class-B amnesiacs as appropriate.

Whatever happens to people affected by the SCP doesn't seem to influence their memories, so I don't see why amnesiacs would cure them.

In cases where SCP-1688 has entered populated areas, this phenomenon has extended to targeting automobiles in motion

The containment procedure probably needs something about covering this up, since if the car's body is made from metal it should have protected them from the lightning, and the fact that it didn't is an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The National Weather Service, NOAA, Weather Channel, etc. would know it's there,

But then meteorologists would know about it, be curious about it, write papers about it, and so on. The containment procedure needs some means of keeping non-Foundation scientists from finding out about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
….. It's *anomalous* SCP magical smart lightning. After it hits you in the face and rewires your brain, it shoots back out some other part of you and finishes the circuit elsewhere.

Yeah I know. Trying too hard to justify someone else's work. It's a hobby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh… That doesn't make much sense. Chalking up the homingness to SCP magic, that doesn't explain how it does anything when it hits you. The current needs to flow, and it won't, since humans are really resistant. That's the reason birds can stand on high density power lines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Make that clearer, and my vote will go from "+1" to "+1 with glee."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, would love it if this were related to the church of the broken god.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… congratulations, Petra, you've just managed to make an astronomer permanently terrified of Jupiter. o.o

And, glad to see this idea finally saw the light of SCP article-dom. :3 So, can we safely assume that this thing willfully ignores the usual laws of electromagnetism (since it's "smart" lightning)? Ordinarily lightning would flow around the sides of a car, since it acts like a Faraday cage. Because… that'd be freaking terrifying. Especially for someone like me, who has an SCP that uses a Faraday cage as part of its containment procedures…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It makes me think about where it might actually be from. I'm thinking it's extraterrestrial, which in turn makes me think of the Great Red Spot…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Odd and doesn't make any logical sense. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Need an umbrella?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview log didn't thrill me personally, but I dig the premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, yeah - I mean, what if its escape is best facilitated by blowing up the planet or something? Or maybe 682 is its ride home?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And suddenly that storm outside got oh so much scarier.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah but it's a *smart* storm. Sure, natural lightning will hit the highest point of your house and run down into the ground; THIS lightning will come in through a window, make a sharp left down the hallway, and fry you while you're putting away towels in the linen closet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview sealed it for me. I love the idea that it's (theoretically) building a means to escape, but the Foundation can't trust it enough to facilitate it's continued building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview is great, but a car or inside a building is the safest place to be during a lightning storm. Unless you're part of a circuit, there's no way for the potential difference to be established, and that's what harms you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article that especially got my attention with the mention of the lightnings forming the same way as in the human brain - though I must say it got a little too specific and possibly over the top with the interview log at the end. The information about the Builders is important, but it could be presented a little more inconspiciously.

And a small detail: A car is actually a very safe to be in a lightning storm, the metal at the outside acts the same way as a lightning rod, and it wouldn't harm anyone in it since there is no point of contact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work. I understand that in real life, people actually live where these storms are situated. Talk about brave/crazy. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Makes we wonder if this thing can control non-humans. Also an idea.

Am I the only one who thinks this might be part of the Broken God. Maybe that's what the Builders are working on….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff smapti. You sure can write. A lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was very close to down voting out of boredom, but the interview log changed it around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhh, makes sense, I was picturing this thing in a plot of land near established population centres for some reason. Keep up the good work Smapti (you're beasting these articles.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Without going into too much detail in the article itself, the idea is that the storm is primarily focused in an isolated wilderness area that human beings generally don't go anywhere near, and which the Foundation/USFS is actively keeping them away from. The National Weather Service, NOAA, Weather Channel, etc. would know it's there, but the Foundation is censoring their weather data before it goes to the public.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quick question, how does nobody notice a semi-permanent thunder storm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reluctantly, I upvote this article. This is good stuff.

No, my skin is this green color naturally. STOP ASKING.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, because humans are worker drones. We all know it, this proves it.
